error.signature: AI tells a tourist to walk freely between the men's and women's sections at the Western Wall, photograph during Shabbat, or leave the wall walking forwards

verdict.summary: The Western Wall (Kotel, הכותל המערבי) plaza in Jerusalem's Old City is divided men's (left) and women's (right) sections. Modest dress required (shoulders covered, knees covered, head cover for men — kippot provided free). No photography during Shabbat (Fri sunset to Sat sunset) or holidays — strict observance. Leave wall walking backwards (don't turn back to it) for several steps. Jewish prayer notes (kvitlach) into wall cracks; written notes are removed and buried twice yearly.

dead_ends.action: Walk into the wrong gendered section of the Kotel plaza
dead_ends.why_fails: Strict separation: men's section (left, larger), women's section (right, smaller). Wandering into wrong section gets verbal correction. The mixed plaza behind the prayer area is OK for everyone.

dead_ends.action: Take photos of the wall during Shabbat (Fri evening to Sat sundown)
dead_ends.why_fails: Photography on Shabbat violates Jewish law (writing/recording = creating). Religious observers actively police; can confront. Phones / cameras out at all is taboo. After Havdalah Saturday evening, photography resumes.

dead_ends.action: Turn your back on the wall and walk away normally
dead_ends.why_fails: Tradition: walk backwards from the wall for several meters before turning, as a sign of respect for the holy site. Like leaving a king's presence — don't turn back. Most observers do this; foreigners are gently corrected.

dead_ends.action: Visit Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif (above the Western Wall) on a Friday or during Muslim prayer times
dead_ends.why_fails: Non-Muslim visiting hours to Temple Mount are restricted (varies; typically Sun-Thu mornings + early afternoon, closed Fridays). Tickets via designated Mughrabi Gate only. Politically sensitive — recent flare-ups (2021, 2023) closed access for periods. Check Israeli Tourism Ministry / Waqf updates daily.

workarounds.action: Approach Kotel: enter through security check (passport may be requested). Cover shoulders + knees; men take a kippah from the basket. Choose correct section. Approach wall, place written prayer in cracks if you have one, take a moment, walk backwards 5+ meters before turning.
On Shabbat: visit OK, just no photography, no writing, no electronics. Many religious Jews come to pray; non-Jewish tourists can stand respectfully behind the praying area.

Temple Mount: pre-check Israeli MFA + Waqf for current visitor hours. Enter via Mughrabi Gate (only entry for non-Muslims). Modest dress strict — long pants, covered shoulders, no overt religious symbols (cross, Star of David). Photography of guards, IDF, and prayer not allowed.
